column2: Fig. 8.3.  The projection of the circles from satellites A (orange) and B (blue) at two times T1 (solid) and T2 (dashed).  The signal at T1 alone fixes the receiver at either R1 or R1’. An additional satellite fix is required to remove the ambiguity. Alternatively, the signal at T2 can be used provided that the receiver is known not to have moved any appreciable distance. In choosing between R2 and R2’, R2 makes better sense.
Fig. 8.4.  A solution on a plane for three satellites.  The three solid circles show constant arrival times for three satellites.  Two solutions are possible.

column2: Figure 9.3. There is no pressure drop across parallel streamlines. (a) the pressure in the side passage at B is the same as the flow pressure at A. (b) the pressure at the exit of a tube at B is the same as the pressure inside the tube at A and also the same as the outside pressure at C. (c) Where flow comes to rest we have a “stagnation point” S where the where the stagnation pressure ps = po + ½ρv2,where v is the flow velocity.

Figure 9.4. Streamlines around a cylinder. (a) The arrows show the direction of the pressure gradient due to centrifugal force. (b) The gauge pressure around the cylinder; red is positive blue is negative.   OUP:  NOT SURE WHERE THESE IMAGES ARE FROM

column2: Figure 9.6. The Coanda effect blowing over a sheet of paper. (a) For flow in a straight line the pressures at A and B are the same. Flow over a curved surface will entrain stationary air from below creating curved streamlines. As we saw in Figure 2, this result in a region of low pressure which pulls the flow downwards. (b) the resulting flow follows the surface. With curved streamlines, the pressure at A is lower than at B, causing lift.

Figure 9.7. Examples of the Coanda effect. (a) a ping-pong ball levitated using a hairdryer (b) a representation of the flow showing the curved streamlines with the centre line of the original flow shown dashed. (c) Water being curved by a spoon. (d) The same effect is responsible for dribbly teapots.

Chapter 10 Coriolis Force and Gyroscopes
Fig.10.1 Eric Laithwaite giving his 1974 Royal Institution Christmas Lectures

Fig. 10.2. (a) A trolley is moving along a radial track on a rotating turntable. It’s speed, v, measured relative to the table is constant but as it moves from P to P′ there is a sideways component of velocity due to the rotation of the table, which reduces with radius. (b) In time t, the trolley has moved from P to P′, but if the track exerted no sideways force on the trolley, it would end up P′′. The sideways force required is called the Coriolis force.

column2: Fig. 10.6. Gyroscopic precession. (a) A spinning bicycle wheel supported on a string does not fall down, as expected intuitively, but precesses around a vertical axis (b) a side view of the bicycle wheel and its axle. The string pushes up with a force mg exactly equal to the weight of the wheel. The resulting moment mgd is responsible for the gyroscopic effect.

Fig. 10.7. A disc spinning in a rotating frame. (a) A rigid frame rotating at angular velocity  contains a disc spinning with angular velocity  The spin axis of the disc is at right angles to the spin axis of the frame. column2: Chapter 12 Why does the shortest day does not coincide with the latest sunrise?
Fig.12.1. Stonehenge    OUP:  NOT SURE WHERE THIS IMAGE IS FROM

Fig. 12.2. The Earth at three points of its orbit A, B and C at time intervals of 23.934 hours. If the earth’s orbit around the sun were circular, then the sun would be overhead every 24 hours. On an elliptical orbit the speed of the earth varies depending on where it is in the orbit, so the timing of “solar noon” varies depending on the time of year.
